sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A sports psychology study measured free-throw accuracy under three self-reported arousal levels before competition: low, moderate, and high. Players attempted 20 free throws after a brief warm-up. Mean accuracy was 58%, 76%, and 63% across the three arousal groups. Heart rate also increased across groups, confirming that the arousal classification captured meaningful physiological differences.
questions.stem: Which arousal level was associated with the best performance?

choices.D: Performance was equal across all arousal levels

explanations.B: Correct. Figure 1 shows the highest free-throw accuracy at moderate arousal, 76%. Accuracy is lower at both low arousal, 58%, and high arousal, 63%.
explanations.A: Low arousal produced the lowest score in the figure.
explanations.C: High arousal was better than low arousal here but still below moderate arousal.
explanations.D: The data show a clear peak at moderate arousal rather than equal performance.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Mean free-throw accuracy under low, moderate, and high arousal levels.

questions.stem: The pattern most directly illustrates which principle?

explanations.C: Correct. The Yerkes-Dodson law predicts an inverted-U relationship between arousal and performance, with moderate arousal supporting the best output. The free-throw data match that prediction closely.
explanations.A: Fundamental attribution error concerns social explanation bias, not arousal-performance curves.
explanations.B: Role exit is a sociological process involving leaving a social role.
explanations.D: The mere-measurement effect concerns attitude and behavior change after asking questions.

questions.stem: Why is heart-rate information useful in this study?

choices.A: It provides converging evidence that the arousal categories reflected real physiological differences
choices.B: It proves that arousal caused every missed shot
choices.C: It replaces the need to measure performance directly
choices.D: It shows that low arousal and high arousal are identical

explanations.A: Correct. Self-reported arousal can be subjective, so accompanying heart-rate differences strengthen measurement validity by showing that the groups also differed physiologically in the expected direction.
explanations.B: Heart rate supports the classification but does not prove that arousal alone caused each shot outcome.
explanations.C: Performance still needs to be measured; heart rate does not substitute for accuracy data.
explanations.D: The passage says heart rate increased across groups, so the arousal levels were not identical.

questions.stem: A coach applying these findings should most likely aim to help players do what before a routine shot?

choices.D: Reach a focused, moderate level of activation rather than being flat or overamped
choices.A: Maximize arousal as much as possible
choices.C: Ignore emotional state because it never affects performance

explanations.D: Correct. Because moderate arousal was associated with the highest accuracy, the best practical strategy is to help athletes reach a controlled, energized state without crossing into excessive tension.
explanations.A: The highest arousal group performed worse than the moderate group.
explanations.B: The low-arousal group also performed worse than the moderate group.
explanations.C: The data show a meaningful relationship between arousal and performance.

questions.stem: Which conclusion is the most cautious and defensible?

choices.C: In this sample, free-throw accuracy was highest at moderate arousal, although the study alone does not show that the same curve applies to every task
choices.A: The study proves all sports performance always peaks at the same arousal level
choices.B: High arousal is universally harmful and should always be avoided
choices.D: Arousal has no meaningful relationship to performance

explanations.C: Correct. The observed pattern supports an inverted-U relationship in this specific context, but generalizing to every sport, person, or task would go beyond the data. The cautious conclusion sticks closely to the measured situation.
explanations.A: The study includes only one task and cannot justify a universal claim across all sports.
explanations.B: High arousal was not uniformly worst and cannot be condemned in all settings from these data alone.
explanations.D: The figure clearly shows a relationship rather than none.

questions.passage: A behavioral economics lab examined delay discounting after a brief stress induction. Participants either completed a timed mental-arithmetic task with evaluative feedback or solved simple practice problems without evaluation. They then chose between smaller immediate rewards and larger delayed rewards. The stressed group chose the immediate reward on 64% of trials, whereas the control group did so on 41% of trials. Self-reported tension averaged 7.6/10 in the stressed group and 4.3/10 in the control group.
questions.stem: Which group chose the immediate reward more often?

choices.C: Both groups chose immediate rewards equally often
choices.D: The figure does not show reward choice

explanations.B: Correct. The stressed group selected the immediate option on 64% of trials, compared with 41% in the control group. The figure therefore shows more delay discounting in the stressed group.
explanations.A: The control group chose the immediate reward less often.
explanations.D: Immediate-reward choices are one of the variables shown in Figure 2.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Immediate-reward choice rate and self-reported tension in the stress-induction and control groups.

questions.stem: The greater preference for smaller-sooner rewards in the stressed group is most consistent with what concept?

explanations.D: Correct. Choosing smaller immediate rewards over larger delayed rewards is the essence of delay discounting. The stressed group’s higher immediate-choice rate indicates stronger discounting of future benefits under stress.
explanations.A: The passage does not assess vocabulary or knowledge accumulation.
explanations.B: Social loafing concerns reduced effort in groups, not intertemporal choice.
explanations.C: The foot-in-the-door effect concerns compliance after an initial small request.

questions.stem: Why does the tension rating matter in interpreting the experiment?

choices.A: It confirms that the manipulation changed participants’ subjective stress state
choices.B: It proves that immediate rewards were objectively larger
choices.C: It removes the need for a control group
choices.D: It shows that every stressed participant made the same decisions

explanations.A: Correct. The higher tension score in the stress-induction group serves as a manipulation check. It indicates that the experimental task successfully increased the subjective state that the researchers intended to manipulate.
explanations.B: Tension ratings do not describe the objective size of the rewards.
explanations.C: A manipulation check complements rather than replaces the control group.
explanations.D: Group means do not imply identical behavior from every individual participant.

questions.stem: A real-world behavior most analogous to the stressed group’s pattern would be which of the following?

choices.B: Choosing a small cash advance today instead of waiting for a larger paycheck next week
choices.A: Preferring a more detailed textbook to a shorter summary
choices.C: Joining a club because friends invited you
choices.D: Taking longer to solve a puzzle after a distraction

explanations.B: Correct. The key pattern is preferring a smaller immediate payoff to a larger delayed payoff. Choosing a cash advance now rather than waiting for more money later captures that same intertemporal tradeoff.
explanations.A: This choice does not primarily involve immediate versus delayed reward.
explanations.C: Joining a club due to social invitation is about affiliation, not delay discounting.
explanations.D: Slower puzzle solving after distraction is a performance issue, not an intertemporal choice.

questions.stem: Which conclusion is most strongly supported by the experiment?

choices.C: The stress induction increased average preference for immediate rewards in this task
choices.A: Stress permanently changes personality traits related to reward
choices.B: The control condition eliminated all impulsive choices
choices.D: Higher tension proves that future rewards have no value under stress

explanations.C: Correct. Because participants were experimentally assigned to stress versus control and the manipulation check confirmed higher tension, the most justified conclusion is that acute stress increased the average tendency to choose immediate rewards in this setting.
explanations.A: The experiment measured immediate decision behavior, not permanent personality change.
explanations.B: The control group still chose immediate rewards on 41% of trials, so impulsive choices were not eliminated.
explanations.D: Future rewards still had value; the stressed group simply discounted them more steeply.
